data.title: Valuations Then And Now: What History Teaches
data.description:
By Jeremy Schwartz, CFA, Bradley Krom, & Brian Manby, CFA

April is recognized as Financial Literacy Month in the U.S., a time to empower investors with the knowledge needed to make more informed, confident decisions. One of the most misunderstood elements of investing is valuation: how much are you paying for a dollar of earnings, and is it worth it?

With tech stocks leading again, many investors are asking whether we're reliving the bubble of 2000. But a closer look at the data reveals a much different picture—and highlights global opportunities that may be overlooked without a solid grasp of valuation fundamentals.

The 2000 Tech Bubble vs. Today's "Magnificent 7"

Understanding valuation is a cornerstone of financial literacy. It's what separates hype from opportunity.

In 2000, companies like Cisco and AOL were trading at valuations that assumed unrealistic future growth—often with price-to-earnings (P/E) ratios north of 100.

Today's "Magnificent 7"—Apple, Microsoft, Nvidia and others—trades at an average forward P/E of 24. Elevated? Yes. But they also have the earnings to back it up. Over the past five years, they've delivered 24% annualized earnings growth, with forward expectations near 16%. That compares to the broader S&P 500, which trades at 21x earnings with growth closer to 13%.

The lesson: high valuations can be justified—but only if earnings growth is real and sustainable.

Figure 1: Price-to-Earnings of the "Magnificent 7"

Sources: WisdomTree, FactSet, S&P. *Calendar year price-to-earnings and earnings growth based on median analyst estimates. **Trailing 3-year where 5-year is not available. Growth is annualized. ***Estimated Long-Term Earnings Growth is annualized and based on median analyst earnings growth estimates over the next three years. Trailing 5-Year Earnings Growth and Estimated Long-Term Earnings Growth as of 3/31/25. All other data as of 4/7/25. Aggregate metrics in italics shown as weighted averages. You cannot invest directly in an index. See the glossary for definitions of terms and indexes.

International Equities: Still on Sale

Another key concept for investors to understand is relative value. Where can your capital potentially go further?

International equities are still trading at a 36% discount to the S&P 500, far wider than the historical median of 17%. Even after a recent rebound, there's room for international stocks to re-rate higher.

Take Japan, for example. Warren Buffett has built a substantial position in Japanese trading firms, which continue to trade at reasonable valuations (~9x–10x earnings) despite delivering strong earnings and dividend growth.

The WisdomTree Japan Hedged Equity Fund (DXJ) is designed to capture similar exposure while reducing the risk of a weaker yen eroding returns—mirroring the currency-conscious approach Buffett employed by issuing yen-denominated debt.

Small Caps: Cheap but Stalled

U.S. small-cap stocks also offer discounts—but without a near-term catalyst. Tariff concerns, weaker earnings momentum and higher economic sensitivity have held them back.

While valuation metrics look appealing, this is a reminder that price alone doesn't equal opportunity. Investors must pair valuation with fundamental and macroeconomic awareness.

Investing Strategy or Portfolio Application

Valuation-aware investors may benefit from reallocating toward global strategies with more attractive pricing and structural support.

Consider these examples:

  • WisdomTree Emerging Markets High Dividend Fund (DEM): Offers an earnings yield of more than 6% and trades at a steep discount. It captures dividend-focused opportunities in regions where sentiment remains overly bearish.
  • WisdomTree Japan Hedged Equity Fund (DXJ): Provides access to Japanese equities with strong fundamentals while managing currency risk.
  • U.S. multifactor and value-tilted strategies: Help mitigate valuation concentration in growth-heavy portfolios and reintroduce diversification across sectors and styles.

These tools offer diversified ways to balance growth potential with valuation discipline and make informed choices grounded in data, not headlines.

Making Sense of Market Signals

Financial Literacy Month can be used as a reminder that investing isn't about chasing trends; it's about understanding them.

Today's market isn't 2000. The "Magnificent 7" have real earnings behind their valuations. At the same time, undervalued international markets, especially in Japan and emerging economies, offer compelling alternatives.

A strong grasp of valuation principles can help investors navigate this environment with greater clarity and confidence. It's not just what you invest in, but why and how, that builds lasting success.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including the possible loss of principal. Foreign investing involves special risks, such as risk of loss from currency fluctuation or political or economic uncertainty. Dividends are not guaranteed, and a company currently paying dividends may cease paying dividends at any time. Due to the investment strategy of these Funds, it may make higher capital gain distributions than other ETFs. Please read the Funds' prospectus for specific details regarding the Funds' risk profile.

DXJ: The Fund focuses its investments in Japan, thereby increasing the impact of events and developments in Japan that can adversely affect performance. Investments in currency involve additional special risks, such as credit risk, interest rate fluctuations and derivative investments, which can be volatile and may be less liquid than other securities and more sensitive to the effect of varied economic conditions. As this Fund can have a high concentration in some issuers, the Fund can be adversely impacted by changes affecting those issuers.

DEM: Foreign investing involves special risks, such as risk of loss from currency fluctuation or political or economic uncertainty. Funds focusing on a single sector generally experience greater price volatility. Investments in emerging, offshore or frontier markets are generally less liquid and less efficient than investments in developed markets and are subject to additional risks, such as risks of adverse governmental regulation, intervention and political developments.

Brian Manby, CFA, Associate, Investment Strategy

Brian Manby joined WisdomTree in October 2018 as an Investment Strategy Analyst. He is responsible for assisting in the creation and analysis of WisdomTree’s model portfolios, as well as helping support the firm’s research efforts. Prior to joining WisdomTree, he worked for FactSet Research Systems, Inc. as a Senior Consultant, where he assisted clients in the creation, maintenance and support of FactSet products in the investment management workflow. Brian received a B.A. as a dual major in Economics and Political Science from the University of Connecticut in 2016. He is holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst designation.

Bradley Krom, U.S. Head of Research

Bradley Krom joined WisdomTree as a member of the research team in December 2010. He is involved in creating and communicating WisdomTree’s thoughts on global markets, as well as analyzing existing and new fund strategies. Prior to joining WisdomTree, Bradley served as a senior trader on a proprietary trading desk at TransMarket Group. Bradley is a graduate of the Wharton School, University of Pennsylvania.

Jeremy Schwartz, CFA, Global Chief Investment Officer

Jeremy Schwartz has served as our Global Chief Investment Officer since November 2021 and leads WisdomTree’s investment strategy team in the construction of WisdomTree’s equity Indexes, quantitative active strategies and multi-asset Model Portfolios. Jeremy joined WisdomTree in May 2005 as a Senior Analyst, adding Deputy Director of Research to his responsibilities in February 2007. He served as Director of Research from October 2008 to October 2018 and as Global Head of Research from November 2018 to November 2021. Before joining WisdomTree, he was a head research assistant for Professor Jeremy Siegel and, in 2022, became his co-author on the sixth edition of the book Stocks for the Long Run. Jeremy is also co-author of the Financial Analysts Journal paper “What Happened to the Original Stocks in the S&P 500?” He received his B.S. in economics from The Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania and hosts the Wharton Business Radio program Behind the Markets on SiriusXM 132. Jeremy is a member of the CFA Society of Philadelphia.

data.title: Labcorp Holdings: Regaining Credibility
data.description:
About a year ago, I believed that there was a healthier diagnosis for shares of Labcorp Holdings (NYSE:LH) with pandemic headwinds moving towards the background.

Shares had been trading around the $200 mark for quite a few years. The business was a modest beneficiary of the pandemic, which boosted demand for its diagnostics solutions. With testing revenues on the decline thereafter, amidst moving parts, the picture was a bit uneven, yet prospects for growth to return were seen in 2024, which made me believe that a valuation multiple re-rating could be in the works.

A Big Lab Player

In the spring of last year, Labcorp announced a bolt-on deal as it acquired the BioReference Health business from Opko Health (OPK). With this $237 million deal, Labcorp was adding about $100 million in clinical diagnostics and reproductive sales, with few other financial details being announced.

Labcorp claims to be one of the largest laboratory service providers in the world, performing hundreds of millions of tests per annum, offering thousands of unique tests for different purposes. The company mainly provides these services to large pharmaceutical names.

Early last year, the company reported a 2.5% increase in 2023 sales to $12.16 billion, with the OPKO deal in the spring of last year adding about a percent to sales. Some three quarters of revenues are generated from Diagnostics Laboratories, with Biopharma Laboratory Services making up a quarter of sales. With million of tests performed, this remains a very labor-intensive business, employing some 60,000 workers.

The company saw adjusted earnings down over three dollars to $13.56 per share, and worse is that GAAP earnings only came in at $4.04 per share, after no less than 9 adjustments made. Net debt was reported at $5.1 billion, ahead of the OPKO deal, for a leverage ratio around 2.5 times based on EBITDA of $2.1 billion.

The company guided for stabilization and recovery in 2024, seeing (adjusted) earnings up to $14.30-$15.40 per share, set to be supported by 5% sales growth. A $210 stock in the spring of last year, traded at just 13-15 times adjusted earnings, but as mentioned, these were adjusted earnings. Growth was set to be return with Covid-19 testing revenues on the decline only seen impacting sales by a percent, as the return of growth might result in valuation multiple inflation.

Modest Progress

Since April of last year, shares of Labcorp traded in a $200-$260 range, now trading at $220 per share, for very modest returns on an annual basis. This followed quite an eventful year, which included multiple bolt-on deals and solid organic growth having returned.

In April of last year, the company was the winning bidder to acquire selected assets of Invitae, a leading medical genetics business. The company paid $239 million for the assets, set to generate $275-$300 million in annual sales.

Based on this acquisition, the company trimmed the earnings guidance to $14.30-$14.90 per share alongside the second quarter earnings report, with the purchase of these assets being dilutive.

In September of last year, the company acquired a 15% minority stake in SYNLAB for an EUR 140 million valuation. This implicitly valued equity of the business at around EUR 1 billion, with SYNLAB being an EUR 2.64 billion revenue business.

In February of this year, Labcorp reported a 7% increase in 2024 sales to $13.01 billion, ahead of the original guidance for the year, aided by the impact of acquisitions announced during the year. Adjusted earnings rose by a dollar to $14.57 per share, in line with the revised guidance, as GAAP earnings improved dramatically to $8.84 per share, doubling compared to the year before with the earnings numbers looking a lot cleaner.

Net debt was down to $4.8 billion, down minimally on the year before, despite the impact of acquisitions, for a leverage ratio in the low 2s. The company guided for solid growth to continue in 2025, with revenues see up over 7% to $13.88-$14.05 billion, with adjusted earnings seen up around 10% to a midpoint of $16.00 per share, plus or minus forty cents.

Delivering

Given the solid growth reported in 2024, and guided for 2025, I think that appeal is increasing quite a bit. Trading at $220, shares trade at 15 times adjusted earnings reported in 2024, and 13–14 times adjusted earnings seen this year. This comes amidst modest deleveraging, with bolt-on M&A deals delivering on real growth.

The company continued with this trajectory, as Labcorp announced another deal with OPKO to acquire other assets of the BioReference Health subsidiary of OPKO. These include laboratory testing activities which focus on oncology and related testing, which contribute $85-$100 million in annual sales. The purchase price is pegged at $225 million, which includes a $32.5 million earn-out provision, with the remainder based on the upfront transaction price.

This again is another bolt-on deal, adding about 0.6-0.7% to pro forma sales, as this fits perfectly in the trajectory and strategy of the business here. Such moves are quite reassuring, as I like the steady focus on organic growth and bolt-on M&A here, amidst reasonable valuations.

All this makes me still rather upbeat on the business here, although that I have some long term concerns as well, driven by the emergence of AI. This could have real impact on some legacy testing methods, as this makes me a bit cautious with regard to the long term outlook for the business model.

Weighing it all together, I am constructive on the business and the valuation, yet fearful about the potential disruption of the business in the long run, and after performing the balancing act I am concluding that I think that shares are largely fairly valued here.

data.title: Azenta: Life Science Business Failing To Come To Life
data.description:
As shares of Azenta (NASDAQ:AZTA) have fallen to fresh 52-week lows, an update on the investment thesis is long overdue. In September of last year, I believed that the life science play was not coming to life, as this trend has continued to today.

Azenta might still be better known as Brooks Automation to long-term investors, as the business changed the name after the divestment of the semiconductor business, thereby becoming a pure play on life sciences. While this strategic reaction looked good on paper, Azenta has been struggling to show organic growth and post profits, leading to shares being down a great deal (albeit from an expensive start).

With a >10 times sales multiple of this business coming down to about 1 times right now, factoring in net cash holdings, expectations have come down, as the company maintains a strong balance sheet. For appeal to be seen, margin growth, and frankly real margins need to be delivered upon, as new management could unleash great value, provided it can deliver.

The Business

After Brooks Automation announced the sale of its semi business to private equity company Thomas H. Lee Partners back in 2021 in a $3 billion deal, the pure play life science business enjoyed real operating momentum. This, and enthusiasm among investors, sent shares up to levels above the $100 mark.

The remaining life science business generated about half a billion in sales, as peak valuations came in at $9 billion, including the net cash position of around $3 billion following the sale of the semi activities, but this still translated into demanding valuations by all means.

Not only was a 12 times sales multiple rather rich, it furthermore was applied to activities which were breaking even at best. Ever since then, the company has made very modest inroads. 2022 sales rose by 8% to $555 million, as adjusted earnings came in at $0.51 per share, these being highly adjusted numbers.

Sales growth accelerated 20% to $665 million in 2023, but this was all driven by dealmaking efforts (supported by the strong cash balances) in combination with share buybacks. On an organic basis, sales were actually down a percent, pressuring margins, with adjusted earnings down to $0.31 per share.

The company guided for 2024 sales up between 5% and 8% on an organic basis to $696-$718 million, and despite the promise of margin gains, adjusted earnings were seen down further to $0.19-$0.29 per share. Through the third quarter, the company announced the departure of its CEO as the company guided for 2024 now down to just $655 million.

With 53 million shares trading at $45 in September, and factoring in a $744 million net cash position, a resulting $1.7 billion operation asset valuation awarded activities a 2.5 times sales multiple. The issue is that of very modest and adjusted earnings, with realistic earnings largely non-existing.

Coming Down Further

Still a $50 stock in February, shares of Azenta have fallen to fresh lows of $25, which looks quite interesting, given the net cash position. In November of last year, Azenta reported a 1% fall in fourth quarter sales to $170 million, with full-year sales down 1% to $656 million as organic revenue growth was down 2%.

In all fairness, this was driven by a 27% fall in B Medical systems sales to $83 million. The company reported adjusted EBITDA of $49 million and adjusted earnings of $0.41 per share, up ten cents on the year before. These, however, remain very adjusted earnings, as the question can be asked if this business was still really profitable. The company aims to "fix" this by announcing the sale of the struggling B Medical systems business.

The company provided somewhat of a comforting guidance for 2025, seeing organic sales up 3-5%, to be accompanied by 300 basis point of EBITDA expansion, but in all fairness, the company has missed on its promises before.

In February of this year, the company reported a 4% increase in first quarter sales to $148 million, with the results no longer including B Medical Systems sales. While EBITDA from continuing operations rose by a full 4 points to 9% of sales, adjusted earnings were flat at $0.08 per share. This number compares to a $0.21 per share GAAP loss, as the discrepancy seems fair, relating to amortization, transformation and restructuring charges, but some of these are quite recurring and involve cash outflows as well.

Continued buybacks meant that the share count is down to 45 million shares, for a $1.12 billion equity valuation at $25 per share. This, however, includes a net cash position of $493 million, for an enterprise valuation of about $630 million. This values the business at around 1 times sales here, but the question is, of course, how profitable these sales are, or better said, can become.

What Now?

With net cash holdings down to $10 and change per share, the operating asset valuation of the firm stands at just around $15 per share, as this represents a life science business valued at 1 times sales, amidst no realistic profitability.

This makes it hard to get upbeat from a pure valuation and earnings potential point of view, as the business is breaking even at best here. That said, a solid balance sheet and the fact that sales multiples have compressed so much means that appeal can be found, but for this to happen, management must deliver on real margin expansion.

While the company anticipates modest advancements, (new) management does not have credibility on its side yet, but there is room for further disappointments, given the strength of the balance sheet. This makes me more upbeat now, purely from a valuation point of view, as the company has seen some operating momentum.

However, for shares to come to life, realistic earnings improvements are required, but the earnings numbers are quite murky. A speculative position might be warranted, based on the belief that management can deliver.

In that, I have no confidence to hold a full position, but right now, the opportunity is arising to take a speculative position here.

data.title: S&P 500: How Bad Could It Get? The Ultimate Worst-Case Scenario
data.description:
High uncertainty

The uncertainty is high, and nobody can really form any meaningful expectations about the near-term future, or even beyond the next 6-12 months.

Thus, it is important to understand all possible scenarios. The best-case scenario is the S&P500 (SP500) has possibly already bottomed out. The ultimate worst-case scenario is that the S&P500 could fall by 70% down to the 2000 level.

All scenarios are tied to the evolving tariff policy and the unfolding trade war, and specifically to the effects on growth and inflation, as well as the general financial stability. More broadly, the main issue is the unfolding process of deglobalization, and the reversal of the post-World-War 2 world order.

The best-case scenario

So, let's start with the best-case scenario. If the trade war de-escalates and the US and China engage in productive trade negotiations, with the hints of reduction in tariffs, the economy could avoid stagflation - inflation might not rise as much and growth could remain positive. Note, this scenario still assumes some tariffs in place, specifically the 10% universal tariff, plus the sector specific 25% tariffs.

It is important to note that over 40% of US inflation is shelter-related. How exactly are the tariffs going to boost rents? The rents are unlikely to be affected by the tariffs, which puts the ceiling on how high inflation could spike in the US, even if prices of some imports spike. Further assuming the global economic weakness, the price of energy could stay low, which should also limit the US inflation spike, despite the tariffs.

It is also important to note that the US is still suffering from labor shortage, which is likely to worsen with the tight immigration policy, in addition to the negative demographics. It's difficult to envision a deep recession without the spike in the unemployment rate. Even with tariffs, the unemployment in the US might not spike due to labor shortage. Thus, the US could avoid a deep recession, and experience only a mild recession or a low growth environment, with elevated inflation.

In this best-case scenario, the S&P500 has likely bottomed at the 4800-5000 level, although the volatility is likely to remain high.

The recession scenario with AI bubble burst

The US could experience a deeper recession, triggered by the negative wealth effect if the AI bubble bursts.

The S&P500 is heavily concentrated in tech mega caps associated with the Gen AI theme. The Gen AI theme is based on the heavy capex spending on data centers and other AI infrastructure/equipment. The first evidence of a reduced AI capex is likely to burst the AI bubble, and this could bring the S&P500 down to the 4000 level as stocks like Nvidia (NVDA) fall, and a deeper recession is triggered.

The recession with the credit event

If the unemployment rate increases, the housing market bubble is likely to burst and cause a credit event - in this scenario the credit spreads would widen as the financial sector experiences turmoil. The housing market has been frozen lately, as people who locked-in low interest rates during Covid refuse to sell, while the buyers remain on sidelines due to high mortgage rates.

The increase in the unemployment rate would force some people to sell into the weakening market, and trigger a steep price drop. Note, the current price-rent ratio is still on par with the 2008 bubble, as the chart below shows.

In this scenario, which also assumes a recession with the AI bubble burst, the S&P500 could fall to the 3000 level.

The worst-case scenario: Capital outflow

The previous scenarios assume that the trade war de-escalates, with some tariffs remaining in place.

The worst-case scenario assumes that the trade war escalates, where the reciprocal tariffs are imposed and trade negotiations are unsuccessful. Further, the US and China situation evolves into a complete separation between the two countries, with respect to flow of goods and capital.

Under this scenario there is a supply shock which could sharply boost the US inflation. In addition, the US economy would slip into a recession, and the AI bubble burst would accelerate. Higher interest rates due to higher inflation would also burst the housing bubble.

However, the key risk under this scenario would be capital outflow out of the US assets, as foreign investors, specifically the Chinese, sell their Treasuries (TLT). This situation could evolve into the downfall of the US Dollar (DXY) as foreign investors start questioning the US Dollar's role as a reserve currency. In this situation, the Fed could even be forced to hike interest rates to protect the Dollar, despite the recession.

The S&P500 could fall below 2000 in this situation, and this is the worst-case scenario - a deep recession, with capital outflow, and the bursting AI and housing bubbles.

Implications

Note, there is also a scenario where the policy narrative shifts to pro-growth agenda, before the negative effects of tariffs kick-in, and the trade war de-escalates. The bull market could continue under this scenario.

However, at this point the volatility is high because the worst-case scenario is unfolding. The bond market experienced a minor turmoil, which forced a temporary delay in the reciprocal tariffs. The bond market stabilized, but the US Dollar continues to depreciate, and this is worrisome.

The market understands that the delay in tariffs is temporary, and the negotiations between US and China have not started. In addition, there are no signs of meaningful progress in trade negotiations with other countries.

The AI bubble continues to deflate with Nvidia's China-related accounting write-off, and this is the early indication of coming next in the tech sector.

However, I am still optimistic that trade war will deescalate, and the volatility in bond market and the US Dollar will subside. But I do expect that the AI bubble is going to continue to deflate, with the reduction in AI capex. Thus, my target for S&P500 is around 4000.

Yet, the first signs that the US labor market is weakening with elevated inflation could start affecting the housing market, which increases the risk of a further drop in the S&P500 to the 3000 level as the housing bubble bursts.

I don't see it as likely that the US Dollar gets seriously challenged as a global reserve currency.

data.title: Equinix: A Good Long-Term Holding Amid Recession Risk
data.description:
I recently published a bullish piece on Germany’s ERP software giant SAP (NYSE:SAP) wherein I announced that I’m revisiting my tech picks here on Seeking Alpha thanks to growing fears of a U.S. and global recession due to trade uncertainties. In the SAP article, I reported that 80% of SaaS companies posted positive revenue growth through the 2008/2009 recession, making the sector an ideal candidate for the current economic environment. Using similar logic, giant data center REIT, Equinix (NASDAQ:EQIX), is another good candidate thanks to having a largely recurring revenue model--I will elaborate on that shortly. Tech tends to fare worse than most sectors during a weakening economic cycle as investors rotate from growth to defensive and value stocks. We are already witnessing that, with the Information Technology sector coming off the first quarter with the second lowest returns, ahead of only Communication Services.

And, red flags are popping up everywhere. The S&P 500 charts formed the dreaded death cross on Tuesday for the first time since March 2022, a deeply bearish signal. Fully 80% of fund managers surveyed in Bank of America's latest Global Fund Manager Survey have singled out a global recession triggered by a trade war as the most significant risk facing markets today--the highest level of consensus in the 15 years BofA has conducted the survey. A week ago, J.P. Morgan predicted a 60% chance of the global economy sinking into a recession by the end of the year, before dialing it back to 40% after U.S. President Donald Trump paused his global tariffs for three months. BlackRock CEO Larry Fink recently says we could very well be in a recession. Meanwhile, Marc Chandler, chief market strategist at Bannockburn Global Forex, says, “What we are going through now is worse than when former President Nixon took us off the gold standard in August 1971.” The dollar index-- a metric that pits the U.S. dollar against a basket of leading world currencies--has pulled back from a 30-month high of 108.30 in December 2024 to 99.13 currently, due to massive dumping of U.S. treasuries amid worsening sentiment on the state of the economy.

On a more positive note, the correction has brought valuations to more palatable levels, with the tech sector currently having a P/E ratio of 35.48 from more than 40 at the beginning of the year. For some context, the sector has averaged a P/E ratio of 25.85-32.78 over the past five years. Valuations for the broader market have also moderated.

Two years ago, I published a bullish Equinix article, touting the company as a growth machine that has been able to maintain steady topline growth through both good and poor economic cycles. Back then, Equinix had logged 80 consecutive quarters of revenue growth, the longest growth streak in the S&P 500. Well, last quarter, the company improved that to 88 quarters, or 22 years of uninterrupted growth.

I did a second article on Equinix a year ago wherein I explained that Equinix was cutting its dependence on leasing data center space from a growing rival, Digital Realty (NYSE:DLR). Unfortunately, EQIX has lagged the market, returning -9.8% from March 2024 compared to -7.9% by the S&P 500. EQIX shares took a big hit after Hindenberg Research published a short report on the company in March 2024, accusing it of selling an “AI pipe dream’’ and manipulating profitability metrics. Hindenburg alleged a lot of things in that report, but the biggest takeaway was that Equinix manipulates its AFFO (Adjusted Funds From Operations) by (mis)classifying certain maintenance expenditures as growth-related Capex. Two months later, Equinix reported that an independent audit had cleared the company of any financial malfeasance or cooking the books.

Unfortunately for EQIX investors, the company still needs to clear the matter in a court of law. Hindenburg--aptly named after the ill-fated hydrogen airship that exploded mid-air in 1937--is perhaps most famous for its damning report on India’s multinational conglomerate, Adani Group, in 2023 that triggered a massive selloff and wiped off ~$150 billion from the group’s market cap. Well, other than underperformance by EQIX, the Hindenburg report led to a class action lawsuit filed by shareholders in June 2024, alleging they bought EQIX at artificially inflated prices over the past five years. Earlier in the current year, a federal judge denied Equinix’s motion to dismiss that lawsuit, leaving EQIX’s fate hanging in the balance. Accounting scandals can be costly: Two decades ago, Enron was forced to pay shareholders a staggering $7.2 billion in partial compensation, in settlement of a $40 billion lawsuit for inflating its profits. I believe this is the biggest headwind facing the company at the moment, and is likely to limit near-term gains for EQIX shares. Then again, on a positive note, Hindberg hung its boots in January, with 40-year-old founder, Nathan Anderson, saying he wants to spend more time with his family. So, EQIX shareholders can rest easy knowing the company is unlikely to be hit with more short seller reports.

Back to Equinix’s financial performance. First off, Equinix has seen a marked slowdown in topline growth, from mid- to low-teens five years ago to high single digits. The company reported Q4 2025 revenue of $2.26B (+7.1% Y/Y), $10M below the Wall Street consensus while Q4 Non-GAAP EPS of $7.92 beat by $5.18. FY 2024 revenue was $8.748 billion, a 7% Y/Y increase while operating income clocked in at $1.328 billion, representing a -8% Y/Y decrease impacted by $314 million asset impairments, restructuring and transaction costs. Thankfully, the company announced that those charges are non-recurring. Adjusted EBITDA was $4.097 billion, good for an adjusted EBITDA margin of 47%, a 160 bps year-over-year improvement. AFFO clocked in at $3.356 billion, good for a 11% Y/Y increase while AFFO per share was $35.02 per share, a 9% increase over the previous year. For full-year FY 2025, Equinix expects revenue to clock in at $9.033 - $9.133 billion, an increase of 3-4% over the previous year on an as-reported basis, or 7-8% on a normalized and constant-currency basis. AFFO is expected to fall in the $3.606 - $3.686 billion range, an increase of 7-10% over the previous year while AFFO per share will clock in at $36.69 - $37.51, a 5-7% Y/Y increase.

Equinix’s revenue growth is lower than the 10.24% average clip posted by the IT sector in the first quarter. I think this is simply the law of large numbers catching up with the company rather than a case of slowing demand. Equinix, Digital Realty and Japan’s Nippon Telegraph and Telephone (NTT) are the global data center and colocation services leaders, accounting for 30% of colocation revenues. Equinix’s global market share of on-ramps to major cloud players provider--central to the AI ecosystem--is nearly 40%. Last year, the company delivered the best gross bookings performing its 26-year history, sealing 16,200 deals across more than 6,000 customers.

Thankfully, Equnix’s margins are well above the 10-25% EBITDA margins typical for tech companies. But here’s the important part: Over 90% of the company’s revenue is recurring, driven by the company’s interconnection franchise. Recurring revenue streams are less susceptible to economic downturns, with the existing subscription base providing stable revenue flows even when new subscriptions adds slow down.

Another positive take: About two-thirds of Equinix’s recurring revenue now comes from owned assets. As I pointed out in last year’s piece, investors tend to overlook the risk posed by Equinix’s earlier model of leasing a large percentage of its data center space from Digital Realty. The once symbiotic relationship between the two companies has deteriorated ever since Digital Realty acquired Telx for ~$1.9 billion in October 2015, with Digital Realty becoming a direct competitor to Equinix. Thankfully, Equinix has managed to cut its reliance on Digital Realty, currently leasing 15 data center locations from the company, down from nearly 30.

Last year, Morgan Stanley picked Equinix among the companies that could benefit as the world upgrades its data centers to handle increased AI workloads. As I reported in that article, there’s been a major shift in data center buildouts over the past couple of years, with providers shifting their infrastructure investment priorities from storage and general purpose computing to AI and accelerated computing infrastructure. Indeed, AI workloads are projected to account for 25% of annual data center capex by 2028. Securities analysts at Bank of America have described it as a “virtuous investment cycle” thanks to artificial intelligence. Global data center capex is projected to hit $200 billion by 2028, good for a robust 18% CAGR, with hyperscalers including Microsoft Inc. (NASDAQ:MSFT), Amazon Inc. (NASDAQ:AMZN), Alphabet Inc. (NASDAQ:GOOG) and Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) representing nearly half of that.

Finally, a couple of weeks ago, Wolfe Research upgraded EQIX to outperform, saying it's "harder to see how EQIX gets more than marginally hurt," in the unfolding economic turbulence thanks to a strong balance sheet, healthy cash balance and low dividend payout. About DeepSeek and cheap AI models threatening the company’s AI prospects, Wolfe’s analyst Andrew Rosivach wrote,

If anything, a proliferation of cheap independent (AI) models could increase the use of data centers for inference (an example of Jevon's paradox). We think Equinix is particularly suited to service inference in retail given main and main locations and the ability for cross connects (particularly to public cloud)."

I couldn’t agree more. For some perspective, EQIX declared $4.69/share quarterly dividend in February, good for 10.1% Y/Y increase. The company also announced plans to increase its dividend by 10% in 2025, marking 10 consecutive years of dividend growth since the company converted to a REIT in 2015. Thankfully, EQIX sports a dividend yield (FWD) of just 2.41%, rather tame for a REIT.

EQIX has handily outperformed its peers over the past decade, and I expect this trend to hold over the next 3-5 years.

data.title: Two BDCs For 60 Cents On The Dollar: 1 To Buy And 1 To Avoid
data.description:
When the risks in the system go up, high return and high yield assets prices tend to go down. The logic behind this is simple: as investors try to hide against the walls of robust and truly defensive securities/businesses, the offense component becomes automatically neglected.

As I have articulated it in some of my previous articles, this dynamic of rising risk premiums (i.e., stronger discounts for the "risk-on" assets) makes total sense. For example, it is only fair that junk bonds or companies that have not yet reached internal cash flow neutrality and instead rely on external financing to execute on the business plan are trading at lower prices than before the tariffs kicked in. Sure, not all of them will fail and, in fact, most of them will nicely bounce back sooner or later, but it is all about the probabilities. It is clear that the sample of underperforming assets and dividends reducing companies has expanded. Hence, the market assigning higher risk premiums to account for that risk is understandable.

In this context, BDCs have been punished accordingly. About two or three months I ago, I remember that the average BDC P/NAV metric stood close to 1.0x. Now, after the recent market sell-off, the average BDC P/NAV has dropped to 0.85x. It tells that on average we can buy a BDC for 85 cents on the dollar.

Obviously, there is a huge variance among the BDCs in terms of what multiples they carry. For instance, Main Street Capital (NYSE:MAIN) trades at P/NAV of 1.65x, while Prospect Capital Corporation (NASDAQ:PSEC) at 0.45x.

In my opinion, BDCs experiencing a ~15% penalty to their valuations due to the unfavorable interplay between their risk profiles (or business models) and the headwinds from the macroeconomic front is actually fair.

However, if we look deeper into the BDC system and start dissecting them one by one, we will see some illogical situations. In other words, there are several names that were already heavily discounted before the tariff issue that have now effectively become businesses with "fire-sale" multiples. For some of them it is just wrong.

In the article below, I will elaborate on 1 BDC, which is a real bargain and on 1 BDC, which, optically, looks like a bargain, but, in reality, is not.

BDC to buy

CION is the only high-risk/high-reward BDC that I own. I have opened an exposure towards CION because I think that the valuations are significantly detached from the fundamentals. Several months ago when I entered CION the gap was wide, and now after the sell-off the gap has become enormous.

Here is how CION has handled the tariff-shock relative to the BDC index (BIZD).

As a result of this, CION is trading at a P/NAV of 0.60x, which makes it the third cheapest BDC. The dividend yield has jumped to ~15%, accordingly.

In my view, paying 60 cents on the dollar is an excellent opportunity for investors to deploy capital into this high-risk/high-reward play. Namely, the risk to reward ratio has become screamingly attractive.

What the market is, currently, saying is that almost half of CION's business will be written down.

While I agree that CION must trade at a discount, I disagree with 40%.

Here are the three most important reasons for this.

First, other BDCs that trade at around the same level of discount have notable allocations into the CLO structures, which inherently come with a penalty to the prevailing fair value. CION has no CLOs. In fact, the lion's share of CION's investments lie in the first-lien zone, which is the safest lever in the BDC space.

Second, CION has a relatively decent quality portfolio. For example, the non-accrual rate stands at 1.4% of the portfolio fair value, which is certainly below the sector average. The portfolio investment company average interest coverage is 2.02x, which is also at a better position than for an average BDC out there.

Third, while CION has a slightly higher debt to equity than its peers, it has almost no issues on the refinancing front as the majority of the outstanding borrowings are based on the floating rate terms. This is a relative advantage.

Having said that, it is of course not just rainbows and roses for CION.

As I said, it deserves a discount.

For instance, the dividend is underwater. The NII per share has declined from the previous quarter due to lower base rates and yield-compression (although, these are sector-wide issues). And, most importantly, a notable chunk of CION's NII per share result comes from PIK or income that is non-cash.

I would say that it is exactly the PIK aspect that significantly depressed the multiple and, among other things, has contributed to a more severe drawdown compared to that of BIZD.

Yet, let's consider these facts:

  • More than 95% of PIK loans are senior secured first-lien.
  • About two thirds of PIK income is structured from the get-go, meaning that it has not occurred due to underperforming investments.
  • Roughly 4% of PIK loans are put into the non-performing category.

I do not know how about you, but for me, putting all of these facts together, a 40% discount seems just way too steep.

BDC to avoid

Logan Ridge Finance Corporation (NASDAQ:LRFC) is another BDC that has been heavily punished by the market. Interestingly, LRFC has lost around one fifth of its market cap just over the past month.

As a result of this decline and the pre-existing discount, the P/NAV has dropped to 0.57x. The dividend yield stands at ~8%. It is this low because almost exactly a year ago LRFC managed to restore some dividend payments and is still, theoretically in a process of building up that capacity to a more competitive level.

However, this is the case, where I do not see a favorable risk to return setup.

I will give you the most important reasons why I think so.

First, LRFC has ~14% of its portfolio located in the equity products from which a sizeable portion is derived from restructured investments (i.e., investments that were initially signed as loans and then due to bad performance restructured into equity). The likelihood of monetizing these investments is quite low, especially in the environment we have now.

Second, about 20% of the portfolio is tied to second lien, subordinated and CLO investments that simply have to come with some form of a discount to account for the lower protection than what traditional first-lien products offer.

Third, LRFC has a debt to equity of 1.22x, which is above sector average and does not give any meaningful room for incremental growth through debt funding.

Fourth, speaking of the growth and the capital to accommodate it, then there is no retained cash flow after netting the dividends and PIK income. So, I do not see how LRFC could achieve some growth from here.

Fifth, and this is a very crucial point, the portfolio quality here is very bad. We can see this from consistently recognized write-downs and emerging new non-accruals. For example, LRFC ended Q4, 2024 with a non-accrual rate of 9%, which is, arguably, one of the highest in the sector. Plus, even though it has been a strong focus point for LRFC to gradually retire the speculative legacy assets, the recent quarter brought another negative surprise, where the Management was forced to yet again realize a loss (about 2.9% from the period start NAV).

So, I do not really see how LRFC could get back on track.

There could, however, be a hope from the merger with Portman Ridge Finance Corporation (NASDAQ:PTMN), but given that this BDC is also experiencing some structural difficulties and trading at one of the steepest discounts in the sector, I would not become too optimistic here.

The bottom line

The market has now assigned higher risk premiums to assets that involve materially higher return opportunities than the risk-free move or high quality blue-chip plays.

One such an example is the BDC space. The average BDC trades now in a notable discount territory.

However, for some BDCs the repricing has been too aggressive.

For example, we should see a 40% discount to NAV for cases that are completely speculative and on the brink of a permanent value destruction.

In this article I discuss two BDCs, which have a similar level of a discount. One of them is, in my view, a clear buy and the other one is not that straightforward pick (i.e., my suggestion on this one is to avoid it).

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: TSMC And Nvidia: Powering The AI Capital Of The World
data.description:
Thesis Summary

As President Trump’s policy reshapes global trade, companies are having to pivot their manufacturing and supply chain logistics.

Taiwan Semiconductor aka TSMC (NYSE:TSM) lies at the center of this whole narrative, due to its role as the main manufacturer of chips and its delicate geographical location in Taiwan.

Nvidia (NVDA), the largest designer of AI chips, is also being caught in the crossfire and now faces new license requirements to sell H20’s to China.

This is why Nvidia and TSMC are partnering up to build AI supercomputers in the United States.

This is very good news for both these companies. Investors should focus on the long-term tailwinds rather than the tariff noise.

TSMC looks like a great buy here based on valuation, technicals and the underlying dynamics on the AI market.

NVDA x TSMC: Match Made In Heaven

According to a recent press release, Nvidia will invest over $500 billion in AI infrastructure in the U.S. over four years.

This will be done with the help of Taiwan Semi, and also other companies in the chip sector like Amkor Technology Inc. and Siliconware Precision Industries Co. (SPIL).

With this move, Nvidia is doubling down on its efforts to localize production to the U.S.

Already, the Blackwell has begun production at TSMC’s fab in Arizona, and production plants are under construction in Texas with the idea to reach mass production within the next 12-15 months.

The engines of the world's AI infrastructure are being built in the United States for the first time,

Source: CEO Jensen Huang.

This is positive news for TSMC, which is not being left out of the equation but rather helping power this move and transition.

With these U.S. investments, TSMC is reducing its exposure to Taiwan and China risks, while also gaining from the massive amount of AI investments that the U.S. is undertaking.

TSMC: Bullish Following Q1

While yes, there is certainly a material risk to tariffs and the current escalation in tension between the East and the West, I would argue investors should focus on the opportunities, just as much as the risk.

There is an opportunity here, for example, for U.S.-based companies to benefit from domestic investments, and there’s an opportunity here for TSMC to benefit from this move too; it already is.

This is supported by its latest Q1 results.

We've seen strong revenue growth in high-performance computing, as well as in Automotive. However, the decline in smartphone sales is concerning, though also seasonal.

The CEO also spoke during the conference call on tariffs and highlighted that they have not yet seen any worrying trends.

"we understand there are uncertainties and risks from the potential impact of tariff policies, however, we have not seen any change from our customers behavior we have so far. Therefore we continue to expect our full year 2025 revenue to increase by close to mid-20s percent in U.S. dollar terms. We might get a better picture in the next few months, and we will continue to closely monitor the potential impact to the end market demand and manage our business prudently."

Source: Earnings Call.

Meanwhile, AI tailwinds continue to be strong, and the company has maintained its guidance for 2025, which is also encouraging.

Valuation

As we move past the Q1 earnings release, TSMC is historically cheap.

Forward P/E is around 17, well below the 5-year average. PEG and forward PEG, my favorite valuation metrics, are both below 1, falling even below the Sector median.

Now, it’s clear that valuation could remain subdued given the current uncertainty. But with every passing day and quarter, the black cloud of tariffs will clear up, and TSMC will emerge stronger.

Technical Analysis

Personally, though, uncertainty remained too high ahead of earnings to make a meaningful bet.

From a technical perspective, we’ve bounced up nicely from oversold territory and even got a recent bullish crossover on the MACD.

However, to buy with high confidence, I’d like to see some form of a bullish divergence, with the price making another low, perhaps into the 61.8% retracement level at $121.

Risks

Needless to say, TSMC comes with significant risk right now.

Over 90% of its chips come from Taiwan. Just a couple of weeks ago, China staged some military exercises around the area.

With Trump and the U.S. now trying their best, seemingly, to alienate China, the country arguably has a lot less to lose from direct conflict with Taiwan.

TSMC is also heavily dependent on a few key customers like Nvidia and Apple (AAPL), with both potentially being big losers from tariffs.

And of course, the current climate has also intensified competition in the fab space, with plenty of competing companies trying to develop their own facilities, which could take business away from TSMC.

Final Thoughts

All in all, this is still a quality company unfairly punished due to the short-term turmoil. These are good levels to slowly DCA into the stock, though I wouldn’t make any big moves ahead of earnings. From a technical perspective, I favor buying into an established uptrend rather than trying to catch a falling knife.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Powell Warns Investors There Will Be No Tariff Bailout
data.description:
The major market indexes struggled at the open after the Trump administration imposed new restrictions on AI-semiconductor exports to China, including Nvidia's lower-powered chips, which the company warned would result in a write-down of $5.5 billion. Consistent with the seesaw analogy I discussed yesterday, a rise in tariffs or trade barriers will lead to a fall in stock prices, as it did yesterday. Yet, the damage was largely confined to the technology sector until 1:30pm. That is when Chairman Powell reminded investors that "tariffs increase costs," and that their current level is much higher than the Fed expected, which means that they will have a much greater economic impact than expected.

He also indicated that tariffs would weigh on the rate of economic growth, which could have labor market implications. This combination hits both sides of the Fed's mandate at the same time. If tariffs run the risk of both higher inflation and slower growth that could lead to a rise in unemployment, the Fed has no choice but to wait and see how the policy impacts the economy. It might be easier if anyone actually knew what the ultimate trade policy would be, but we don't. This wasn't new news, but investors reacted as though it was, sending stocks in a tailspin. What was new is that Powell indicated he would not bail out the stock market anytime soon from its fear-driven decline over trade policy.

This is not what investors wanted to hear yesterday, especially with volatility levels elevated and the market's recovery so fragile, but Powell's commitment to the Fed's mandate is a certainty that I think investors should welcome. His comments will certainly not be welcomed by the president or administration officials, but he is in the driver's seat at the Fed until March 2026, so it really doesn't matter. I am glad he made his intentions clear because I think it will force the market to work even harder to effect the change in policy we need to avert a recession in 2025. That change should now come sooner rather than later.

It is no coincidence that big market declines like the one we had yesterday are immediately followed by reports of "big progress" on trade agreements. The one that came after yesterday's close related to talks with Japan, which is now subject to just a 10% levy, as well as 25% on cars, steel, and aluminum. Any form of an agreement with such a large trading partner should be a template for others, which should also bring down volatility and support risk asset prices. The Trump administration is in just as much of a hurry as our trading partners to avoid the "reciprocal" tariff rates scheduled to be implemented within 90 days. Administration officials know the economic consequences would be cataclysmic for the US economy if those punitive rates went into effect, and it would be a widely reported self-inflict disaster with no one else to blame.

I think the market senses this, which is why volatility levels are starting to subside. While still elevated, the Volatility Index (VIX) has come well off the extreme fear levels of two weeks ago. If we can bring it down below 30, that increases the likelihood that we are still in a bull market that just experienced a deep correction. The longer volatility remains elevated, the more likely we will see rough waters ahead, as we did in 2022. The good news is that we should see trade deals in rapid fire over the coming weeks, which, I think, will be viewed positively by investors.

I think that takes us back to the 5,700 level on the S&P 500, which implies upside of approximately 7%. Along the way, I will be reassessing how much damage has been done to the economy over the past six weeks from trade policy negotiations and implementations, as well as any long-term implications. My base case is still that the economic expansion will continue and the bull market, while wounded, is still intact.

data.title: REIT Executives Ponder: 'Blue Skies Ahead?'
data.description:
We had the opportunity to attend the NYU REIT Schack Symposium held last week in NYC. With over 40 REIT executives speaking at this one-day event, it is truly one of the best REIT events of the entire year.

The major topics that were covered at the event focused on 4 items:

  1. Tariffs
  2. Interest Rates
  3. AI and the Digitalization of Real Estate
  4. Growth and Consolidation

Panel 1: Market Dynamics – Blue Skies Ahead

Seth Weintrob, MD and Global Head of Real Estate, Gaming and Lodging Morgan Stanley

Leslie Hale, President and CEO, RLJ Lodging Trust

Ronald Havner, Chairman of the Board, Public Storage

Mark Parrell, President and CEO, Equity Residential

Owen Thomas, Chairman and CEO, BXP

The first panel started with the question at hand regarding uncertainty and volatility impact on their respective businesses and customers. In addition, the topic of demand demographic and drivers was examined along with what Sovereign Wealth Funds (SWF) are thinking when it comes to investing in USA real estate.

Leslie Hale highlighted that though there has been some softness, the business customer has been resilient as it continues to recover, though they are seeing softness in group business following DOGE cuts where properties in SoCal and DC have seen that weakness. She notes that overall demand has “hung in there” and the desire to travel remains strong, but there is limited visibility looking out into the summer as market impact affects disposable income and the ability for folks to travel.

Owen Thomas commented that office leasing is correlated to in-person work and corporate health and though they haven’t seen an impact on DC yet, they do expect corporate earnings growth will be impacted and note that the in-person work pickup through DOGE mandates bringing employees back to the office along with government contractors at work could provide tailwinds.

Owen also examined how important the USA is in the overall world of commercial real estate, as approximately 70% of the market cap in the world of global real estate is in the USA; thus, we are the driver. He highlights how SWFs make decisions with long-term views rather than what has happened over the last couple of days. Since the USA makes up so much of the global market of real estate, where else will the SWFs invest? Owen also highlighted how they have the ability to raise debt capital and with a laddered debt maturity schedule, they can finance properties over time but with wide credit spreads it will affect those who need to raise capital as the cost of borrow continues to go up.

Mark Parrell noted that their long-term view of housing remains bullish, as we still have a massive undersupply of housing (to the tune of 4-7 million units short). He said that supply is declining in some markets, while occupancy in NY is 98% and DC is currently at 97%. He also discussed the concessions that are being seen in TX markets such as Dallas and Austin but with so much uncertainty, it tends to lead to less speculative development. In addition, the question remains to what happens when more supply comes online with less demand in some markets that leads to compounding concessions? With Fannie/Freddie as a potential backstop, he noted that the long-term outlook affects short-term balance sheet opportunities, and you have to question when to lean into those opportunities.

Ron Havner commented that the issues in the housing markets are good for the storage industry as people need space. He said, “Storage is a space substitute” and noted that the markets that were in demand during COVID are the ones seeing headwinds such as Florida and Texas. Pricing is challenged in those markets that accelerated during COVID.

With tariffs impact, Leslie Hale noted that it has a 2-prong impact on the cost of renovations and the impact on the supply chain, which hinders the ability to do renovations. She noted that if you aren’t already underway with a renovation project, then it will most likely be delayed. Tariffs have created a negative sentiment on international travel, and notes the recent data on Canada to USA travel declining.

All the executives noted that the key to surviving the rollercoaster in these cycles is to have the balance sheet that can withstand volatility and thus being low levered helps to weather the storm.

Panel 2: Repositioning REITs for Growth

Thomas Grier, MD and Global Head of Real Estate, Gaming and Lodging Investment Banking at JPM

Scott Brinker, President and CEO, Healthpeak Properties

Christina Chiu, President, Empire State Realty Trust

Kristin Gannon, Managing Director, Eastdil Secured

Adam Portnoy, President and CEO, The RMR Group

Stephen Yalof, President and CEO, Tanger

The second panel started with a discussion looking at private capital markets activity along with cost of capital. Each panelist agreed that having access to private capital makes sense for the right opportunities, but the key is to have scale/size and the balance sheet to be able to raise capital. The discussion about “scaling the company” was very informative as it impacts the ability to raise capital, grow your presence in the sector, and give you something to shoot for in growth. Look at the growth in data centers, or Blackstone has an example. Another topic of discussion of total return projections from investors as the general consensus was “high single digits should be acceptable while active investors may want a higher amount” but the key is looking at the dividend yield along with stock growth which could be impacted over the next 6–10 months based on investor sentiment. Many companies agreed that it is hard to sit on the sidelines when seeing opportunities, while long-term investors' sentiment may be changing.

Kristin Gannon noted that private capital is important to REITs on a go-forward basis including off-balance sheet JVs. With potential challenges to bring in core capital, she noted that insurance capital is becoming more active in the space along with foreign capital investors finding opportunities with “best in class” REITs while Canadian investors are backing away from US investments due to political pressures.

Adam Portnoy noted the importance of private capital in their portfolio as they are seeing demand from private capital in housing/senior housing, retail, industrial and lending and sees them growing more capital in the private markets. He noted that the cost of capital is much different for a small cap REIT versus large cap REITs as investor return expectations are increasing and that is why they have pivoted towards raising private capital in the wrapper to put it at the asset level.

Scott Brinker highlighted looking at the business from multiple angles as he noted that they are a healthcare company rather than a real estate company and the importance of culture, mindset and being able to deliver. He said that the company has a 4 million sf land bank in Cambridge and San Francisco as a large developer of lab space, and they are able to take advantage of development opportunities as fundamentals change. When looking at their merger with DOC, they saw it as an opportunity to add value and vertically integrate the platform along with multiple synergies. He noted that it's great continuity for both companies as “It’s a merger and not an acquisition.” With potential capital strategy changes in a volatile market, he noted that it is important to match fund effectively as new development in outpatient business is attracting capital and a wide investor base. With some potential distress in the life science space, he notes that there could be more allocating capital there in the future in a debt with path to ownership structure.

Christina Chiu spent time talking about the ESRT strategy in capital recycling and redeployed their capital proceeds into NYC residential and Williamsburg retail. They will always be NYC focused due to “live work play visit” and a key driver of earnings is the Observatory with about 50% of the visitors coming from foreign countries. And though too early to make a long-term prediction, she also highlighted the decline in recent bookings from foreign travelers to the USA. She notes that the cost of capital is challenging on the equity side as they are open to working with JV capital and see different opportunities in office, retail and multifamily.

Stephen Yalof highlighted the SKT strategy driven by its core thesis of “leasing, leasing, leasing” since there is not a lot of new retail space being added in the market. As a result, he noted that “our retail space is more valuable than the day before” as an aggressive leasing team can work on getting top dollar for the most desirable real estate and the company is always trying to find the proper mix of the right size tenant and tenant mix and the ability to replace retailers with new opportunities. He looked at being able to underwrite investments as an operating company and look for healthy retailers where you can buy a deal at an 8 cap and underwrite at a 10 cap which provides that opportunity. He also noted with the recent volatility that many retailer brands have seen these cycles before and many of these tenants have 10-year leases in place at their properties.

Panel 3: Opportunities in a Volatile Interest Rate Environment

Jeffery Horowitz, Global Head of Real Estate, Gaming & Lodging Investment Banking, BofA Securities

Kenneth Bernstein, President and CEO, Acadia Realty Trust

Colin Connolly, President and CEO, Cousins Properties, Rob Harper, Head of Real Estate Asset Management, Blackstone

Tammy K. Jones, Co-Founder and CEO, Basis Investment Group

Sam Landy, President and CEO, UMH Properties

Dana Petitto, Chief Operating Officer and Portfolio Manager, Brookfield REIT

The third panel highlighted the various opportunities that the companies have seen as Ken Bernstein highlighted both cyclical and secular tailwinds over the last year as street level retail remains popular and noted some of their recent investments. He said that the lack of new supply plus the rotation of retailers moving out of whole towards direct to consumer along with the omnichannel world is where the store is the more profitable channel and thus retailer demand will drive those future tailwinds. He also noted the importance of finding motivated sellers ready to transact as it is important to examine what the length of the capital investment will be. He said that they haven’t seen signs that retailers are slamming the brakes on new leasing and thinks that tariff impact to retailers and potential cost impact to consumers should be “manageable” but the key factor to watch is the tariff impact on the jobs market and potential fallout.

Colin Connolly focused on current office building supply plus the little development that is out there means that there is negative supply with office demand accelerating. As public and private markets converge, it means that the public companies will be able to acquire private market assets at good values and looked at their 2 recent deals as prime examples of how this plays out. He noted that the recent capital markets shock is leading to uncertainty and questions.

Tammy Jones looked at the rotation of Veris out of office into now a pure-play multifamily REIT and focus on the markets that they operate in with millions of dollars of assets potentially for sale this year. She noted that it is important to invest in markets that hold up irrespective of the asset class. It is important to maximize value and close the discount to NAV by using the private markets. She noted that uncertainty is a time for caution as there will be forced sellers which will create investment opportunities. She looked at the flight to quality on investments along with migration trends noting that its “a tale of 2 cities” and it's important to examine those trends.

Rob Harper provided very valuable insights considering Blackstone’s position in the industry as he noted that inflation helps them to be able to “look around the corner” to make the best investment decisions. With 23 portfolio companies here in the USA, the company is getting real-time info across many of their platforms and highlighted the importance of communication across their teams. He highlighted the volatility that is occurring on a sector-by-sector basis, along with the cost to underwrite developments week over week. He said they look for opportunities where the platform scale drives growth and returns, using data centers and student housing as examples. He said that good real estate is always a good investment since it is tangible. Not surprising, but Rob said that unlike M&A opportunities in the past, they are looking at other deals and JVs with REITs or potentially buying assets directly from REITs as there will be a lot of opportunities in the near future.

Dana Petitto analyzed the strong fundamentals overall in real estate plus the lack of new supply is providing optimism and investment opportunities in 2025 and with so much chaos out there right now, things are currently on pause as they wait for things to shake out, but they do see plenty of opportunities in housing and other sectors that could withstand what will potentially play out. She looked at housing across multiple states and said that rental housing is indeed driven by the supply/demand imbalance along with the generational shift.

Sam Landy spent much of his presentation highlighting the value proposition of both manufactured housing and UMH. He noted that factories continue to get more efficient every year which continues to lead to more cost savings. He said millions of baby boomers need rising income and inflation creates opportunities for REITs to raise dividends plus the liquidity of REITs provides investors those benefits. He examined how supply constrained we are for housing and focused on the thousands of acres they own adjacent to their owned properties and sites which will provide future opportunities to develop thousands of new homes. He also examined the JV they have with Nuveen plus their Opportunity Zone platforms that provide growth opportunities.

Panel 4: Investor Roundtable

Mary Hogan Preusse, Senior Advisor, Fifth Wall

Brian Jones, Managing Director, Neuberger Berman

Matthew Kirschner, Senior Vice President and Portfolio Manager, Cohen & Steers

Cedrik Lachance, Director of Research, Green Street

Nick Joseph, Head of the Real Estate and Lodging and Global Real Estate Super Sector, Citi

Suzanne Sorkin, Senior Vice President, Adelante Capital Management

The fourth panel provided great investor insights from a variety of REIT investors looking at REIT performance versus the S&P along with cash flow growth in the sector, leverage, activism, fund management business, share buybacks and ESG issues.

Matthew Kirschner noted recent performance compared to tech stocks (or the Mag 7) which has driven some of this valuation gap, and it is expected that there should be some moderation of earnings growth, but he said that REITs are not designed to be hyper growth return companies and there is nothing broken with the REIT model. When examining JVs, he noted that too many JVs could be detrimental, and it's important to make sure that interests are aligned along with mechanisms or levers to pull to be able to exit the JV.

Cedrik Lachance looked at public to private valuations in regard to NAV and mentioned that the NAV signal is important, but it is only one tool in the valuation of REITs as you have to also look at the long-term value of real estate, the balance sheet, G&A structure, quality of the management team and corporate governance when it comes to valuation. He said there is no correlation between short-term earnings growth and the long-term valuations of REITs. He noted that activism is important in the REIT industry, and it ensures that management teams are acting in the best interest of investors as management teams have improved over the years, but feels that there is a lot of work to be done. He noted that activism and short sellers are beneficial to the market as it leads to accountability and better results by the companies.

Nick Joseph noted that M&A is somewhat tied to NAV valuations but with so many REITs trading at discounts to M&A, it seems that the market is on pause for a bit. He noted that 10 of the 80 companies in their coverage universe were in play for M&A deals (the 11th being HR, but with the new CEO appointment this week, that was removed from the list). When looking at JVs and fund management business, he said that leverage is very important, and you have to be mindful of the risks and examine it on a use-case perspective.

Brian Jones said that NAV is just one out of a number of factors that they examine, and he noted that a question to ask is what sectors investors are comfortable to deploy capital into and which management teams should you trust to deploy that capital. He examined the disconnect in cap rates analysis due to NAV where Listed REITs tend to lead the private markets and thus the question of where values are going. He said REITs provide that income offer along with an obsession for growth and with the sector currently yielding around 4.5%, he feels that the yield should continue to improve over time as cash flow improves. When looking at the fund management model, it said that it's important to time the comp structure of the management team versus its fund business as fund management business could work, but it does lead to complexity and governance issues. When it comes to activism, Brian commented that it is important to engage with the activist investors to understand their reasons since investors are voting on board members and strategy.

Suzanne Sorkin noted that both public and private real estate should perform similar over time. In addition, she highlighted the importance of strict corporate governance, looking at Prologis fund business as a long-standing model going back to their core DNA from the AMB days. When it comes to ESG, she felt that shareholders and management teams are intertwined but for difference reasons. A great quote: “Governance matters until it no longer matters” and she thinks that REITs will continue to pursue excellence on the ESG front.

Panel 5: Innovation, Prop-Tech & AI in the REITverse

Sherry L. Rexroad, Chief Financial Officer, Piedmont Office Realty Trust

Conor Flynn, CEO, Kimco Realty Corporation

Andy Power, President and CEO, Digital Realty

Dallas Tanner, Chief Executive Officer, Invitation Homes

Brendan Wallace, Chief Executive Officer and Chief Investment Officer, Fifth Wall

The fifth panel focused on AI and prop-tech in the REIT industry, as Brendan Wallace noted that AI has improved everything, and it is the next industrial revolution that is changing demand for space. The million-dollar question is looking at how AI will change real estate and the commercialization of space. The issue is that there is not enough energy to fuel the energy powering AI, and thus have to build out new energy sources and have access to that energy. He notes that data centers use 3% of the current power supply, which is forecasted to grow to 20% in the next 15 years. Brendan highlighted the economics of AI which are better, faster, cheaper, and easier. AI forces companies to move to the cloud, and we are seeing venture capital investors pouring huge sums of money into large language models that quickly become out of favor or obsolete in a short amount of time. He says it’s the reason why companies like Amazon and Microsoft lead that space, due to their scale and size. As a result, many companies are benefiting from the growth of the industry at the expense of venture capital funds. As someone who is deeply working with many real estate companies, Brendan wasn’t shy in sharing his thoughts that AI is top of mind for every single real estate owner these days as companies examine how the AI-powered economy is going to change different demands in terms of both space and sectors.

Andy Power knows a thing or two about data centers and said that AI has been an accelerant of data center growth as there is a long tailwind of demand as applications intermingle. He noted that DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) is opening the doors to more innovation and technology and commented about what Google Maps and other applications have done for other industries such as Uber/Lyft and DoorDash compared to the yellow pages, since “technology builds on itself.” Andy also commented that you have to fine tune the key ingredients of the power equation and the platform which can drive earnings growth. The faster response time leads to building longer-term relationship with customers, and it's important to be flexible. Echoing what others have said throughout the day, “don’t lock yourself into one model or platform, but be flexible.”

Dallas Tanner commented that at this point, AI and data is a slow process, but they are rolling it out more and more and see value in using it for backtesting as a way to improve things going forward. He said that AI is making things much faster on the front end of the business, and it is helping with evolving strategies across other parts of the business. He said the key thing is to focus on efficiencies and revenues (aka the low-hanging fruit) plus AI gives them opportunities to be innovative and think quicker. With AI constantly evolving, you don’t have to be tethered to the first idea.

Conor Flynn shared an interesting anecdote that made me think of The Shawshank Redemption when he said, “If you’re not using AI today, then you’re being left behind.” He said that AI chatbots are getting much more sophisticated, where 90% of deals being done are by a chatbot on the residential side. He thinks that AI will get more efficient on the retail side as it helps them figure out the highest and best use case of real estate. He looks at recent innovations with ChatGPT as far as modeling of vacant parking lot space but echoed how important it is to “be adaptable and embrace change.” With these models, it's important to use as many different programs as possible (as he mentioned that also use Copilot) as good data going into the system gets you good data coming out of the system as well.

A Conversation with Hamid Moghadam, Chairman and CEO of Prologis

Hamid spent time-sharing the growth of the Prologis platform, their fund management business and the potential impact of tariffs.

He cited the sole reason for the growth of AMB and Prologis was the importance of understanding your customers and their needs. You need to know what questions to ask your customers, and every single innovation is driven by a customer need. He used examples of their deals with L'Oréal and Webvan back in the day as responding to customer needs and then how this development benefits other partners down the road. He noted that by building sustainably and operating it effectively helps it build upon itself and create revenue streams and other opportunities.

When it comes to tariffs, he thinks that things will be figured out or the alternative could be Armageddon. He looks back at other macroeconomic cycles and what was learned in the past, using COVID as an example. He thought companies would have learned about proper supply chain and inventory management after ships being stuck unable to offload cargos in port and as a result companies carried more inventory for 6 months after COVID, but now retailers are back in the same position as before where they are lean on the inventory side.

Hamid spent time discussing the 3 Cs of their business: Customers, Change through Innovation and Operational Excellence and Culture. He said it's all in how you hire, recruit, and motivate. For example, he said he personally calls every single employee on their 10/15/20/25-year anniversaries and makes it a point to visit every one of their 80 offices in a 2-year cycle. He said it is important to spend time with your employees, and they will appreciate it since culture is the only source of a successful competitive long-term competitive advantage.

Panel 6: Keynote M&A and Strategy Panel – Offense, Defense, Growth

Mike Graziano, Co-Head of the Global Real Estate Investment Banking Business, Goldman Sachs

Jackson Hsieh, President and CEO, Macerich

Matthew Lustig, Chairman of Investment Banking North America/Head of Real Estate and Lodging, Lazard

Edward Pitoniak, Chief Executive Officer, VICI Properties
